IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important problem (morpheme segmentation) and introduces a principled semantic signal (vocabulary-wide difference vectors) that meaningfully reduces over-segmentation and attains state-of-the-art on MC and the new SD17 dataset. By proposing a new dataset (SD17) and a semantically grounded validation of orthographic rules, it is likely to change practice and open productive research directions combining distributional semantics with morphological induction. However, from a theory-first perspective the reliance on greedy inference without formal guarantees and restriction to concatenative morphology limit its transformative potential, keeping the contribution at strong but not revolutionary impact. | B(4): This paper tackles an important practical problem—accurate morpheme segmentation—by introducing a novel, vocabulary-wide syntactico-semantic signal (difference vectors) combined with orthographic rules, which demonstrably reduces over-segmentation and yields state-of-the-art results including on the new SD17 dataset that specifically addresses compositionality issues. The approach and the SD17 resource are likely to change how researchers validate segmentation (moving beyond local semantic checks) and will be broadly useful to practitioners building morphological analyzers or lemmatizers for concatenative languages. Limitations (greedy inference error propagation, restriction to concatenative morphology, and modest dependency on a small gold tuning set) constrain universality but do not materially reduce the method’s practical impact or reusability.

By proposing a new dataset (SD17) and a semantically grounded validation of orthographic rules, it is likely to change practice and open productive research directions combining distributional semantics with morphological induction. However, from a theory-first perspective the reliance on greedy inference without formal guarantees and restriction to concatenative morphology limit its transformative potential, keeping the contribution at strong but not revolutionary impact.

The approach and the SD17 resource are likely to change how researchers validate segmentation (moving beyond local semantic checks) and will be broadly useful to practitioners building morphological analyzers or lemmatizers for concatenative languages. Limitations (greedy inference error propagation, restriction to concatenative morphology, and modest dependency on a small gold tuning set) constrain universality but do not materially reduce the method’s practical impact or reusability.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and readable: the intro, method (rule learning + semantic validation), experiments (datasets including new SD17) and conclusion follow a clear logical flow and the main contributions are stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples (e.g. segmentation examples, comparisons to Morfessor and LLSM) are informative. Writing quality is good with thorough related-work coverage, though the presentation could benefit from a bit more explicit discussion of the greedy inference failure modes and a clearer pointer to where exactly semantic checks intervene (minor clarity gaps for a theory-minded reader). | B(4): The paper appears well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (MORSE approach, vocabulary-wide semantic checks, and the new SD17 dataset). Notation and methodological description are described as consistent and thorough (rules, semantic checks, inference procedure), and the figures/tables/examples seem informative for evaluating segmentation choices and results across MC/SD17 variants. Writing quality is reported as good, with only minor presentation issues (e.g., a few places where the greedy inference limitations and tuning-set dependency could be more explicitly emphasized), so I judge it as well-written with small opportunities for clarifying specific caveats.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper offers a notable novel perspective by integrating vocabulary-wide syntactico-semantic signals (difference vectors from word representations) with orthographic rule learning for morpheme segmentation—a step beyond prior work that used only local semantic checks. This is a non-trivial extension of existing segmentation approaches (e.g., Morfessor and prior semantic checks) and the authors clearly differentiate their contribution (the global difference-vector validation and the new SD17 dataset). From a theory-first standpoint it is not a radical new formalism but a meaningful and informed methodological advance rather than a mere engineering combination, so I judge it as moderate-to-high novelty. | B(4): The paper proposes a clearly new angle by integrating vocabulary-wide syntactico-semantic difference-vector signals with orthographic rule discovery for morpheme segmentation, which is distinct from prior works that used only local semantic checks. Introducing the SD17 dataset to address compositionality failures in existing benchmarks is a substantial resource contribution that strengthens the methodological claim. The combination of rule learning plus semantic validation and the reported ability to extract lemmas are non-trivial extensions over prior morpheme segmenters, so the work would meaningfully inform experts even if it builds on existing components.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper offers a notable novel perspective by integrating vocabulary-wide syntactico-semantic signals (difference vectors from word representations) with orthographic rule learning for morpheme segmentation—a step beyond prior work that used only local semantic checks. This is a non-trivial extension of existing segmentation approaches (e.g., Morfessor and prior semantic checks) and the authors clearly differentiate their contribution (the global difference-vector validation and the new SD17 dataset). From a theory-first standpoint it is not a radical new formalism but a meaningful and informed methodological advance rather than a mere engineering combination, so I judge it as moderate-to-high novelty.

Introducing the SD17 dataset to address compositionality failures in existing benchmarks is a substantial resource contribution that strengthens the methodological claim. The combination of rule learning plus semantic validation and the reported ability to extract lemmas are non-trivial extensions over prior morpheme segmenters, so the work would meaningfully inform experts even if it builds on existing components.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The empirical evaluation is strong: it introduces a new SD17 dataset addressing compositionality, compares to multiple baselines (Morfessor, LLSM variants, Base Inference) and reports precision/recall/F1 at boundary and morpheme levels, and includes hyperparameter sensitivity (performance stabilizes at ~300 gold segmentations). However, from a theory-first perspective the paper lacks formal justification or guarantees for the greedy inference and no provable properties (e.g., convergence/consistency) are given; ablation detail on core components (e.g., contribution of difference-vector checks vs orthographic rule filtering) is not clearly stated in the summary. The authors do acknowledge key limitations (greedy error propagation, restriction to concatenative morphology), so failure modes are discussed, but deeper robustness analyses and more principled theoretical treatment are missing — hence a strong but not exhaustive evaluation. | B(4): Experiments are strong: multiple baselines (Morfessor variants, LLSM, Base Inference), evaluation on standard MC and a newly proposed SD17 dataset that addresses compositionality, and reported gains including state-of-the-art results. The paper documents hyperparameters and reports sensitivity to gold tuning size (steady at ~300), and discusses concrete limitations (greedy inference error propagation, restriction to concatenative morphology). Missing from the provided summary is an explicit ablation isolating the contribution of each semantic signal (e.g., difference-vector vs local checks) and no mention of released code for full reproducibility, so while thorough overall there are minor gaps that prevent a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The empirical evaluation is strong: it introduces a new SD17 dataset addressing compositionality, compares to multiple baselines (Morfessor, LLSM variants, Base Inference) and reports precision/recall/F1 at boundary and morpheme levels, and includes hyperparameter sensitivity (performance stabilizes at ~300 gold segmentations). However, from a theory-first perspective the paper lacks formal justification or guarantees for the greedy inference and no provable properties (e.g., convergence/consistency) are given; ablation detail on core components (e.g., contribution of difference-vector checks vs orthographic rule filtering) is not clearly stated in the summary. The authors do acknowledge key limitations (greedy error propagation, restriction to concatenative morphology), so failure modes are discussed, but deeper robustness analyses and more principled theoretical treatment are missing — hence a strong but not exhaustive evaluation.

reviewer_b.justification: Experiments are strong: multiple baselines (Morfessor variants, LLSM, Base Inference), evaluation on standard MC and a newly proposed SD17 dataset that addresses compositionality, and reported gains including state-of-the-art results. The paper documents hyperparameters and reports sensitivity to gold tuning size (steady at ~300), and discusses concrete limitations (greedy inference error propagation, restriction to concatenative morphology). Missing from the provided summary is an explicit ablation isolating the contribution of each semantic signal (e.g., difference-vector vs local checks) and no mention of released code for full reproducibility, so while thorough overall there are minor gaps that prevent a top score.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses a core NLP problem with a novel semantic+orthographic method, strong empirical evaluation, and a useful new dataset, making it a good fit for a top venue. While Reviewer A notes modest methodological limitations (greedy inference and concatenative focus), these are acknowledged by the authors and do not outweigh the clear empirical contributions and reproducibility of the work, so I award the top score.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP problem (morpheme segmentation) with methods (semantic word representations combined with orthographic rules), a new dataset (SD17), and standard baselines/metrics, so it is well within the scope of a top NLP/ML venue. From a theory-first perspective I note methodological weaknesses the authors themselves admit — greedy inference without theoretical guarantees and restriction to concatenative morphology — which do not preclude publication but justify a minor scope concern. The work’s empirical rigor (multiple datasets, canonical variant, hyperparameter reporting) and claim to be the first to use vocabulary-wide difference-vector signals make it relevant to the venue’s audience, though stronger theoretical analysis or robustness/ablation studies would improve fit further.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles a core NLP problem (morpheme segmentation) with a novel semantic+orthographic method that yields state-of-the-art results and introduces a reusable semantically-driven dataset (SD17). The methodology and evaluation follow community standards (multiple baselines, boundary/morpheme-level metrics, clear hyperparameter reporting) and the work is directly relevant to researchers building morphological analyzers and representation-aware NLP systems. Practical limitations are acknowledged and the demonstrated adaptability (lemmatization) and empirical gains align well with the venue’s priorities for reproducible, impactful resources and experiments.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper's empirical claims are supported by experiments and reasonable baselines, and they acknowledge key limitations (greedy inference error propagation, concatenative-only scope). The main weaknesses are methodological: lack of statistical significance testing, limited ablation studies, and absence of formal theoretical guarantees, which are important but do not overturn the experimental support. Given the solid empirical evidence and the issues being mostly gaps in analysis rather than correctness flaws, a score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are empirically supported: experiments show improved F1 over Morfessor and others and a new SD17 dataset addresses compositionality issues. However, there is no formal theoretical justification or guarantees for the proposed semantic validation or the greedy inference procedure, and the authors acknowledge error propagation from greedy segmentation. Experimental methodology appears broadly sound (multiple baselines and datasets) but the summary lacks mention of statistical significance testing or thorough ablations isolating core components, leaving some concerns about robustness and unsupported assumptions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments: MORSE is evaluated against reasonable baselines (Morfessor, LLSM, etc.) and shows state-of-the-art performance on MC and the new SD17 datasets, and the authors acknowledge and analyze key limitations (greedy inference error propagation, concatenative-only scope). No complex mathematical derivations are needed and none appear incorrect. My concerns are limited to missing details about statistical significance of improvements and code release/reproducibility, and the need for a small gold tuning set (reported but not fully quantified), which are minor methodological gaps that do not overturn the empirical claims.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper compares against a strong, relevant set of baselines on standard and new datasets using consistent metrics, and that the empirical comparisons are comprehensive and well-controlled. Reviewer A’s minor concern about hyperparameter tuning and greedy inference affecting comparability does not undermine the overall fairness of the comparisons and can be addressed with a clarification or ablation. Given the thorough baselines, metrics, and discussion of limitations, the comparison merit is excellent.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a strong set of relevant baselines (Morfessor variants, LLSM 2015, Base Inference) and evaluates on standard and newly proposed datasets using boundary- and morpheme-level precision/recall/F1, which indicates largely fair head-to-head comparisons. The authors also describe the distinguishing novelty (vocabulary-wide syntactico-semantic difference-vector checks) and report when alternatives approach performance, and related work coverage is reported as thorough. My remaining concern is minor: hyperparameter tuning on a small gold set and greedy inference choices could affect comparability unless identical tuning regimes were applied to baselines — an issue that should be explicitly clarified or ablated, but does not invalidate the overall comparative effort.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a strong and relevant set of baselines (Morfessor, LLSM, Morfessor S+W variants, and Base Inference) on standard (Morpho Challenge) and a newly introduced SD17 dataset using the same boundary/morpheme-level precision/recall/F1 metrics. Comparisons appear fair and well-controlled (same datasets and metrics), and the authors explicitly discuss when prior methods are comparable and enumerate their method’s advantages and limitations (e.g., greedy inference, concatenative-morphology focus). Given the thorough related-work coverage and inclusion of direct, state-of-the-art baselines, the empirical comparison is comprehensive and appropriate.

main_claims: Presents MORSE, a morpheme segmentation framework that combines semantic information from word representations with orthographic features to reduce over-segmentation.
First to consider vocabulary-wide syntactico-semantic (difference-vector) information for morpheme segmentation rather than only local semantic checks.

Introduces a new semantically-driven dataset (SD17) addressing compositionality issues in existing benchmarks and shows improved performance over Morfessor and other published baselines, achieving state-of-the-art on evaluated datasets.
MORSE can identify lemmas as part of its iterative segmentation process and is adaptable to lemmatization/stemming.
_summary.methodology: MORSE learns candidate affix transformation rules from orthographic word pairs, then validates/infilters those rules and word pairs using semantic signals from word representations (difference vectors and cosine similarities); inference greedily selects validated add/replace rules (Radd then Rrep) to segment words.
limitations: Greedy inference can propagate early segmentation errors leading to subsequent mistakes.
Limited to concatenative morphology, decreasing efficacy on languages with non-concatenative morphology.
Requires a small gold tuning set for hyperparameter tuning (though authors report minimal dependency; steady performance reached at ~300 gold segmentations).

reviewer_a.name: Theory-first Rigourist
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP/ML researcher with a focus on formal models of morphology and provable properties of algorithms; 15+ years reviewing for ACL/ICML.
traits: penalises papers lacking formal theoretical justification or guarantees for algorithmic claims (e.g., convergence, consistency) — expects at least discussion or bounds
penalises methods that rely on heuristic or greedy inference without thorough ablation/robustness analyses (requires experiments that isolate the contribution of core components)
requires clear comparison to classical morphological models and analysis of failure modes (will deduct points if only empirical wins are reported without error analysis)
prefers principled modeling over engineering tweaks — will downgrade papers whose main novelty is combining existing features unless accompanied by new theoretical insight
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the paper provides principled, generalizable understanding and rigorous evaluation; skeptical of empirical-only improvements and looks for theoretical grounding and detailed ablations.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical Innovator
reviewer_b.background: Applied NLP scientist with experience building morphological analyzers and resources for low-resource languages; frequent program committee member for EMNLP/CoNLL.
traits: rewards clear empirical advances and new datasets that fix known benchmark shortcomings — gives credit for well-motivated, reusable corpora
favors methods that effectively combine semantic representations with orthographic signals and will upscore papers demonstrating state-of-the-art gains across multiple datasets
rewards practicality and reproducibility: expects code release, stable training/inference procedures, and demonstrations of adaptability (e.g., lemmatization extension)
gives positive weight to novel, problem-driven ideas (e.g., vocabulary-wide syntactico-semantic difference-vector usage) even if formal guarantees are absent
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Prioritizes empirical impact, dataset quality, and practical novelty; inclined to accept papers that provide strong experiments, new resources, and clear improvements in real tasks.
